questions.prompt: Which of the following correctly describes the AD curve and explains why it is downward-sloping?
choices: AD is downward-sloping because higher output reduces expected prices.
AD is downward-sloping because higher prices reduce real money balances, raise interest rates, and reduce spending.
AD is downward-sloping because firms' costs rise with output, forcing prices down.
AD is downward-sloping because higher prices attract more foreign demand.
questions.explanation: AD slopes downward through the real money balance channel: when P rises, M/P falls, pushing up the interest rate (via IS-LM), which reduces total spending and equilibrium output.

questions.prompt: In 2020, COVID lockdowns caused both output and inflation to fall sharply. Which type of shock does this represent, and why?
choices: A supply shock, because prices fell.
A demand shock, because output and prices moved in the same direction.
A supply shock, because the shock originated in the goods market.
A demand shock, because lockdowns are exogenous to the firm.
questions.explanation: When Y and P move in the same direction, it indicates a demand shock. The COVID lockdowns shifted AD left (reduced consumer spending and investment), lowering both output and prices together.

questions.prompt: Which of the following would represent a supply shock in the AD-AS framework? Select all that apply.
choices: A rise in oil prices that increases production costs at every level of output.
An increase in government spending that boosts aggregate demand.
A spike in wage expectations that shifts SRAS up/left.
A monetary expansion that increases the money supply.
questions.explanation: Supply shocks shift SRAS directly. A rise in oil prices raises production costs at every level of output (option 0), and a spike in wage expectations raises the costs firms expect to pay, shifting SRAS up/left (option 2). Options 1 and 3 are demand shocks that shift AD.

questions.prompt: The 2022 energy spike after Russia's invasion of Ukraine caused output to slow while inflation accelerated. According to the AD-AS model diagnostic, what type of shock was this?
choices: A demand shock, because inflation accelerated.
A supply shock, because output and inflation moved in opposite directions.
A demand shock, because output fell.
A supply shock that could have been offset by fiscal expansion.
questions.explanation: The lesson's diagnostic rule applies: when Y and P move in opposite directions, it was a supply shock. The energy spike raised production costs, shifting SRAS left, raising prices while output fell.

questions.prompt: How do demand shocks and supply shocks differ in their policy implications?
choices: Demand shocks require supply-side reforms; supply shocks can be offset by monetary policy.
Demand shocks are easy to respond to with monetary or fiscal policy; supply shocks are harder because expanding demand worsens inflation.
Supply shocks are costless to manage; demand shocks require painful recessions.
Both require the same policy response regardless of the shock's origin.
questions.explanation: The lesson contrasts the two: demand shocks can be offset by shifting AD back via monetary or fiscal policy. Supply shocks force a trade-off because expanding AD to restore output only adds inflation on top of the cost-push effect.
